title: Drug delivery device
abstract: Embodiments of the invention include devices, compositions and methods for the controlled release of therapeutic substances, such as drugs. Control over the rate of release of the therapeutic substances from the devices is achieved by the use of nanoporous membranes in which the pore size is matched to the molecular diameter of the therapeutic substances. Some embodiments of the invention achieve zero-order release by the use of membranes with a pore diameter that is more than five times the Stokes' diameter of the therapeutic substance released.
claims: Claims WHAT IS CLAIMED IS: 1. An implantable device for zero-order release of a therapeutic agent having a molecular diameter, the device comprising: a capsule suitable for implantation; a reservoir encapsulated by the capsule, the reservoir suitable for containing a pharmaceutically acceptable formulation of the therapeutic agent; and at least one nanoporous membrane in fluid contact with the reservoir, wherein the nanoporous membrane comprises nanopores having diameters more than 5 times greater and up to 19 times greater than the molecular diameter of the therapeutic agent. 2. The implantable device of claim 1, wherein the nanopores have a monodisperse distribution. 3. The implantable device of claim 1, wherein the nanopores have a polydisperse distribution. 4. The implantable device of any one of claims 1-3, wherein the nanopores have a unimodal distribution. 5. The implantable device of any one of claims 1-3, wherein the nanopores have a polymodal distribution. 6. The implantable device of any one of claims 1-5, wherein the nanopores in the nanoporous membrane provide a cross-sectional surface area open for mass transport, and wherein at least 97 percent of the cross-sectional surface area is provided by nanopores with a diameter, which is greater than 5 times and to about 19 times the molecular diameter of the therapeutic agent. 7. The implantable device of any one of claims 1-6, wherein the average diameter of the nanopores is between 6.5 and 7.4 times the molecular diameter of the therapeutic agent. 8. The implantable device of any one of claims 1-7, wherein the molecular diameter is the Stokes' diameter of molecules of the therapeutic agent. 9. The implantable device of any one of claims 1-8, wherein the nanoporous membrane is a titania nanotube membrane. 10. The implantable device of any one of claims 1-9, wherein the pharmaceutically acceptable formulation comprises a solid state form of the therapeutic agent. 11. The implantable device of any one of claims 1-10, wherein the pharmaceutically acceptable formulation comprises the solid state form of the therapeutic agent suspended in a liquid carrier. 12. The implantable device of any one of claims 1-11, wherein the therapeutic agent is one of a peptide and a protein. 13. The implantable device any one of claims 1-12, wherein the therapeutic agent is a GLP-1 agonist. 14. The implantable device of claim 13, wherein the therapeutic agent is exenatide. 15. The implantable device of claim 14, wherein exenatide is present in an amount between about 60 micrograms and about 50 milligrams. 16. A method for treating a disease in a subject in need thereof, the method comprising: administering to the subject an implantable device for zero-order release of a therapeutic agent, the device comprising: a capsule suitable for implantation; a reservoir encapsulated by the capsule, the reservoir suitable for containing a pharmaceutically acceptable formulation of the therapeutic agent; and at least one nanoporous membrane in fluid contact with the reservoir, wherein the nanoporous membrane comprises nanopores having diameters more than 5 times greater than the molecular diameter of the therapeutic agent. 17. The method of claim 16, wherein the device is implanted subcutaneously. 18. The method of any one of claims 16-17, wherein the device is implanted non-surgically by means of a hollow implantation needle. 19. The method of any one of claims 16-18, wherein the average diameter of the pores is between 6.5 and 7.4 times a molecular diameter of the therapeutic agent. 20. The method of any one of claims 16-19, wherein the molecular diameter is a Stokes' diameter of molecules of the therapeutic agent. 21. The method of any one of claims 16-20, wherein the nanoporous membrane is a titania nanotube membrane. 22. The method of any one of claims 16-21, wherein the pharmaceutically acceptable formulation comprises a solid state form of the therapeutic agent. 23. The method of any one of claims 16-21, wherein the pharmaceutically acceptable formulation comprises the solid state form of the therapeutic agent suspended in a liquid carrier. 24. The method of any one of claims 16-23, wherein the therapeutic agent is one of a peptide and a protein. 25. The method of claim 24, wherein the therapeutic agent is a GLP-1 agonist. 26. The method of claim 25, wherein the therapeutic agent is exenatide. 27. The method of claim 25, wherein exenatide is present in an amount between about 60 micrograms and about 50 milligrams. 28. The method of any one of claims 16-27, wherein the disease is type 2 diabetes. 29. The method of any one of claims 26-28, wherein the mean steady-state plasma concentration of exenatide is 170 pg/ml to 600 pg/ml. 30. The method of claim 29, wherein the mean steady state plasma concentration of exenatide is 170 pg/ml to 350 pg/ml. 31. The method of claim 29, wherein the mean steady state plasma concentration of exenatide is 170 pg/ml to 290 pg/ml. 32. The method of any one of claims 16-31, wherein the therapeutic agent provides therapy from 30 days to about 1 year.
